hook: A jewel of Polish post-war modernism — the swooping concrete-and-glass commuter station built into the Vistula escarpment.
facts: Designed by Arseniusz Romanowicz and Piotr Szymaniak, the architect duo behind much of Warsaw's cross-city railway line (linia średnicowa).
The plastic, sculptural form of the pavilions dates to 1954-55, with interiors completed 1960-62; the upper ticket pavilion has a distinctive parabolic concrete shell roof.
It is entered in the register of monuments (rejestr zabytków) for heritage protection.
The former lower ticket pavilion now houses the cult bar/klubokawiarnia 'Warszawa Powiśle' at ul. Kruczkowskiego 3B.
It sits midway up the Warsaw escarpment, beside the pre-war Poniatowski Bridge approach.
legend: Nicknamed a 'perła na szynach' (pearl on the rails) by architecture writers.

sources.note: Monument register entry; gives architects, coordinates 52.2337N 21.0281E (corroborates coords).
Architectural history, Romanowicz & Szymaniak, modernist form.
Powojenny Modernizm catalogue: dates and escarpment siting.

w: A 'pearl on the rails' — Romanowicz and Szymaniak's commuter station built into the Vistula escarpment, its upper pavilion a swooping parabolic concrete shell from the late 1950s. A high point of post-war Polish modernism, listed now. The old lower ticket hall has become a cult riverside bar, Warszawa Powiśle. Catch a train, or a beer.

hook: The storied inner-city ground of Polonia Warszawa — wooden-tribune origins from the 1920s, ringed by Socrealist stands.
facts: Built 1926-28 on ul. Konwiktorska to modified plans by architect Juliusz Nagórski; opened 15 August 1928, official opening 30 September 1928.
After WWII a Socialist-Realist multi-purpose clubhouse (1954) with a representative entrance from Bonifraterska was added alongside new stands.
Bears the name 'im. gen. Kazimierza Sosnkowskiego'; current capacity around 7,150.
Address ul. Konwiktorska 6, in the Nowe Miasto (New Town) area of Śródmieście; unusually close to the Old Town for a football ground.
legend: During the 1944 Warsaw Uprising the stadium served as a launch point for an (unsuccessful) insurgent operation.

sources.note: History, 1928 opening, 1954 Socrealist building, coords 52°15′20″N 21°00′04″E = 52.2556, 21.0011 (corroborates coords).
Stadium dossier: capacity, address Konwiktorska 6.
Press: the ~100-year history at Konwiktorska.

w: The inner-city ground of Polonia Warszawa, opened in 1928 a stone's throw from the Old Town — unusually central for a football stadium. After the war a socialist-realist clubhouse rose alongside the stands. In 1944 insurgents launched a failed Uprising attack from here. Still home to the Black Shirts, by the New Town.

hook: Warsaw's main river port — an early-20th-century basin off the Vistula, now a marina for rowers, kayakers and a moored cultural barge.
facts: A river port on the left bank of the Vistula, at the 511th km of the navigable Vistula route, along ul. Solec and Czerniakowska.
The basin was shaped at the start of the 20th century and held a shipyard that repaired and built river vessels; modernised by 2014.
The bosmanat (harbour-master) sits at ul. Solec 8 by the port gate; the port now serves yachts, kayakers and the Warszawskie Towarzystwo Wioślarskie (rowing club).
The barge 'Herbatnik' is moored here, where the 'Ja Wisła' foundation runs cultural events.

sources.note: History, function, coords 52°13′18″N 21°02′50″E = 52.2217, 21.0472 (corroborates coords).
City Zarząd Zieleni: shipyard origin, 2014 modernisation, current uses.

w: Warsaw's river port, a basin cut off the Vistula at the start of the 1900s where a shipyard once built and mended river boats. Today rowers and kayakers share the water, and a moored barge, the Herbatnik, hosts cultural nights by the harbour-master's gate. The landlocked city's maritime corner. Watch the river traffic from the quay.

hook: Warsaw's biggest and best-known Vistula river beach — wild sand on the right bank beneath the Poniatowski Bridge.
facts: The largest and most recognisable city beach on the Vistula, on the right (eastern) bank beside the Poniatowski Bridge.
Has beach-volleyball courts, deckchair hire, and in summer food kiosks; the city built a dedicated pavilion beside it.
Sits directly opposite the National Stadium (PGE Narodowy), with sweeping city views.
The free 'Pliszka' ferry links it across the Vistula, stopping by a pier of the Poniatowski Bridge.

sources.note: Right-bank location by Poniatowski Bridge, near National Stadium, ferry.
Guide: largest Vistula beach, amenities.
Geocode = 52.2367, 21.0424 (corroborates coords).

w: The Vistula's biggest city beach — wild sand on the far bank, right under the Poniatowski Bridge and straight across from the National Stadium. Deckchairs, volleyball nets and beer kiosks in summer; the free little Pliszka ferry hops you over the water to reach it. Warsaw's seaside, hundreds of miles from the sea.

hook: Corazzi's neoclassical temple of money on Bank Square — once home to the Bank Polski and the Warsaw stock exchange.
facts: Built 1825-28 to a design by Antonio Corazzi (with Jan Jakub Gay), at plac Bankowy 1.
Housed the Bank Polski (central bank of the Congress Kingdom) and the Warsaw Merchant Exchange / Giełda; the exchange moved out in 1877 and Bank Polski was liquidated in 1886.
In the 1863 January Uprising insurgents seized the Main Treasury of the Kingdom held in the bank's vaults.
Destroyed in WWII and reconstructed 1950-54 under Piotr Biegański; it now houses an art museum (Carroll-Porczyński / John Paul II Foundation collection).
legend: In August 1944 the building was the northernmost insurgent redoubt of the Old Town during the Warsaw Uprising (the 'Reduta Bank Polski').

sources.note: Corazzi 1825-28, bank+exchange history, coords 52°14′31″N 21°00′08″E = 52.2421, 21.0024 (corroborates coords).
Reduta Bank Polski during the 1944 Uprising.

w: Antonio Corazzi's neoclassical temple of money on Bank Square, built in 1825 for the Bank Polski and the exchange. In the 1863 uprising rebels seized the Kingdom's treasury from its vaults; in 1944 it held out as the Old Town's northernmost insurgent redoubt, the 'Reduta Bank Polski.' Flattened in the war, rebuilt as an art museum.

hook: The cinematheque of the Polish national film archive — repertory classics screened in the restored 1950s rotunda of the old Kino Stolica.
facts: Belongs to Filmoteka Narodowa (now FINA); began in 1956 attached to the Warsaw film archive.
Since 2009 it occupies the listed 1950s building of the former Kino 'Stolica' at ul. Narbutta 50A, reopened after renovation in November 2012.
Has two screening rooms (260 and 47 seats); the heritage 'Stolica' hall (263 seats) has a stage and orchestra pit — Warsaw's first cinema with dedicated musicians' space.
Its archival-cinema status flows from Filmoteka Narodowa holding one of Europe's largest film collections; it hosts the Święto Niemego Kina (Silent Film Festival).

sources.note: History, move to the listed former Kino Stolica at Narbutta 50A, two halls.
Filmoteka Narodowa archive cinema; coords 52°12′20″N 21°00′38″E = 52.2056, 21.0106 (corroborates coords).

w: The cinematheque of Poland's national film archive, screening repertory classics in the restored 1950s rotunda of the old Kino Stolica on Narbutta. Its main hall, with a stage and orchestra pit, was Warsaw's first cinema built for live musicians. Silent-film festivals, and the past on the big screen.

hook: A tucked-away matcha bar, tea room and organic-tea shop on quiet Oleandrów — handmade wagashi and proper whisked matcha.
facts: Combines a matcha bar, an intimate tea room and an organic-tea shop, run by the Moya tea brand.
Located at ul. Oleandrów 6, on a quiet Śródmieście street hidden between Polna and Marszałkowska, about 5 minutes' walk from Politechnika metro.
Makes wagashi (Japanese sweets) on-site daily; staff explain culinary vs ceremonial matcha and correct preparation.

sources.note: Description as Japanese tea house + shop at Oleandrów; wagashi, matcha focus.
The Oleandrów matcha bar interior/concept.
Geocode of Oleandrów 6 = 52.2169, 21.0183 (corroborates coords).

w: A hidden matcha bar and tea room down quiet Oleandrów, where the Moya tea people whisk proper matcha and make wagashi fresh on the premises each day. They'll walk you through ceremonial versus culinary grades and how to whisk it right. Japan, tucked between Polna and Marszałkowska. Sit in the back room and slow down.

hook: A 350-metre pedestrian passage threading behind the 1960s Centrum department stores — Warsaw's modernist 'Eastern Wall' alley.
facts: Pedestrian/commercial passage running ~350 m from ul. Widok to ul. Sienkiewicza, behind the Wars-Sawa-Junior department stores.
Built 1963-69 as part of the Ściana Wschodnia (Eastern Wall) complex; originally 'Pasaż Śródmiejski'.
Renamed in 1997 after Stefan Wiechecki 'Wiech', the Warsaw writer-satirist; earlier given a name in 1970.
Lined by landmarks including the Atlantic cinema (Chmielna 33), the former Relax cinema, the Zodiak architecture pavilion, and a memorial stone to August Agbola O'Brown.

sources.note: Route, 1963-69 Eastern Wall build, naming, coords 52.2327N 21.0110E (corroborates coords).
City district page on the passage.

w: A 350-metre pedestrian passage threading behind the 1960s department stores of the Eastern Wall, from Widok to Sienkiewicza — modernism's answer to an arcade. The Atlantic cinema opens off it; a stone remembers August Agbola O'Brown, a Nigerian-born Varsovian of the Uprising. Named for Wiech, the city's satirist.
